description: The flagged line only calls VendorCacheManager.getInstance() to obtain a cache manager instance; there is no hashing, cryptographic, or message-digest operation anywhere in the shown code, so this is a rule mis-classification rather than a weak-hash usage.

Calendar.getInstance() is a standard JDK date/time API call with no cryptographic or hashing function involved; the rule mis-tagged it as a weak_hash finding purely because 'weak_hash' pattern matching triggered on an unrelated Calendar API call used to compute a ship date.

java.util.Currency.getInstance() is a JDK API for looking up currency metadata by ISO code; it performs no hashing or cryptographic operation, so this is a rule mis-classification rather than a weak-hash vulnerability.

The reported line is `throw new ServiceException(e);` inside a catch block, with no HTML/UI sink or tainted data flow present anywhere in the shown code; the surrounding method builds a Zone map and description list, not markup. This is a mis-classification by the tool, not a genuine XSS sink.
NumberFormat.getInstance is a locale-aware number formatting utility, not a cryptographic hash function; this is a mis-classification by the rule, not a weak-hash vulnerability (CWE-328).

The flagged line only calls VendorCacheManager.getInstance() and cacheManager.getManager().stop() during servlet context teardown; there is no hashing, cryptographic, or password-related function invoked here, so the weak_hash/CWE-328 classification is a mismatch against unrelated cache-lifecycle code.

The origin header value is passed via HttpServletResponse.setHeader, and modern Servlet containers reject/strip CR/LF characters from header values, so classic HTTP response splitting/header injection is not achievable here. The real issue is that the code reflects an arbitrary request Origin into Access-Control-Allow-Origin, which is a permissive CORS misconfiguration (CWE-942/CWE-346), not header injection as classified.

The reported sink is httpResponse.setHeader (an HTTP response header), not a SQL query or statement — there is no SQL sink anywhere in this code, so the CWE-89 classification is a mis-tagging. The origin header does reflect into a CORS header unsanitized, which could be a permissive-CORS issue (CWE-942), but that is a different vulnerability class than what was flagged here.

This is the getHeader override of an XSS-sanitizing request wrapper; it calls cleanXSS() on the raw value before returning it, so it is the sanitization point itself, not an HTML sink — the flagged line performs mitigation rather than introducing a vulnerability.

The endpoint declares produces={"application/json"} and returns a JSON-serialized AuthenticationResponse, not an HTML sink, so there's no browser HTML rendering path for reflected XSS; additionally the value returned is a freshly signed JWT (refreshedToken) generated by jwtTokenUtil.refreshToken, not raw attacker-controlled content reflected verbatim.

Calendar.getInstance() merely creates a calendar object for date arithmetic (computing a default 24-hour date range); it has no relation to hashing or cryptographic functions, so this is a rule mis-classification, not a real weak-hash vulnerability.

This is a Spring REST controller method annotated with @ResponseBody returning a POJO that gets serialized to JSON (not rendered into an HTML template/view), so there is no HTML sink here despite the tainted data flowing to the return statement; XSS via CWE-79 requires an HTML rendering context which this JSON API endpoint does not provide.

This is a Spring REST endpoint returning a JSON body via ResponseEntity.ok(new AuthenticationResponse(...)); there is no HTML sink or template rendering here, so CWE-79 (XSS) is a misclassification. Additionally, the value returned is a freshly generated refreshedToken, not the raw tainted header value.

The origin header value is reflected into the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header without CRLF filtering, but Java Servlet container implementations (Tomcat, Jetty) reject/strip CR/LF characters in setHeader values, preventing classic HTTP response-splitting/header-injection. The real defect here is a CORS misconfiguration (reflecting arbitrary Origin with Allow-Credentials: true, CWE-942), not CRLF header injection as classified.

The reported line sets an HTTP response header (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) from the request's Origin header; there is no SQL query or database sink anywhere in the shown code, so this is a tool mis-classification (likely reflected-header/CORS issue at most, not CWE-89 SQL injection).

Calendar.getInstance() is a date/time utility used here to add seconds to a Date for a password-reset grace period; it has no relation to hashing or CWE-328 (weak hash), so this is a tool misclassification, not a real vulnerability.

The username is extracted from the JWT presented in the Authorization header and logged via string concatenation without any newline/control-character sanitization, allowing an attacker who controls their own username (or forges a token claim) to inject CRLF sequences and forge log entries before the token is even validated against user details. No encoding or stripping of the value occurs prior to the logger.info call.
Calendar.getInstance() is a date/time API call with no cryptographic or hashing function involved; this is a rule misclassification, not a weak-hash vulnerability.

The shown code only returns an unsanitized value derived from the X-Forwarded-Host header (attacker-controllable); the actual HTML sink where this return value is rendered is not shown here, so reachability/exploitability of an XSS cannot be confirmed from this snippet alone.

The shown code only extracts and returns the client IP as a string from request headers; there is no HTML/output sink in this method at all, so the flagged line cannot itself be an XSS sink. Any actual XSS risk would depend on unseen downstream code that writes this value into an HTML response without encoding.

The reported source is itself session data (request.getSession().getAttribute) flowing back into another session write, so there's no actual crossing of a trust boundary — both ends are within the same session context; `language` here is a normalized value produced by languageService.toLanguage(locale), not raw unvalidated attacker input.

The reported line merely returns a `Language` object obtained via `languageService.getByCode(lang)`/`defaultLanguage()`; there is no HTML rendering or output-writing sink in the shown code, so this is not an XSS sink and the tool appears to have mis-tagged a return statement as a sink.
NumberFormat.getInstance is a locale-aware number/decimal parser, not a cryptographic hash function; the rule mis-classified an unrelated API as weak_hash (CWE-328 does not apply here).

The reported sink is System.out.println, which writes to the server console/log, not to an HTTP response body rendered as HTML; there is no browser-reflected output here, so this is not a valid XSS sink despite the tainted parameterValue.

Collator.getInstance is a locale-aware string comparator used for sorting/collation, not a cryptographic hash function; CWE-328 (reversible/weak hash) does not apply to this API at all, so this is a rule mis-classification.

This line only constructs a java.util.Currency constant from Locale.US; it contains no hashing, cryptography, or randomness logic whatsoever, so CWE-328 (use of weak hash) is a clear mis-classification by the tool.
This is a constant defining a template placeholder key name (used for email template variable substitution), not an actual hardcoded password value; the string value equals the constant's own name.
This is a constant defining a string key name ("ORDER_ID_TOKEN") used as a map/session attribute identifier, not a hardcoded credential, password, or secret.

This security filter chain is scoped to /auth/** and uses stateless authentication (httpBasic + jwtCustomerDetailsService, SessionCreationPolicy is stateless in typical JWT configs) rather than cookie/session-based auth, so CSRF (which relies on browsers automatically attaching session cookies) is not applicable — disabling it here is standard practice for token-based REST APIs. Without evidence this endpoint relies on cookie-based session state for authorization, this is not an exploitable CSRF
BEARER_TOKEN is just the literal string "Bearer " used as an HTTP Authorization header prefix for parsing, not a credential, secret, or password; it contains no sensitive value.
The flagged line is a constant filename string for an email template ("email_template_password_reset_request_user.ftl"), not a hardcoded password or credential; CWE-259 does not apply.
The flagged line defines a constant holding a Freemarker email-template filename, not a password or credential; this is a mis-classification with no hard-coded password present.

This is a regex pattern for validating password complexity (uppercase, lowercase, digit, length 6-12), not a hardcoded credential; CWE-259 (use of hard-coded password) does not apply to a validation pattern string.
The flagged line defines a constant holding a Freemarker template filename for password-reset emails, not a hardcoded credential; CWE-259 (hardcoded password) is a mis-classification here.

The excelFile parameter is passed unsanitized into ResourceFactory.newClassPathResource, allowing path traversal/arbitrary classpath resource loading if this method is reachable from user input (e.g. via a REST controller as in known SalesManager/Shopizer path-traversal reports); no validation or path normalization is present in the shown code.

getKieSession(Resource dt) writes an externally-supplied Resource into a KieFileSystem and compiles+executes it as Drools rules, which can run arbitrary Java/MVEL code — a classic code-injection sink if dt originates from user-controlled data; the shown code doesn't confirm the caller's trust level, but the sink pattern itself is dangerous by design.

criteria.getCriteriaOrderByField() is concatenated directly into the HQL 'order by' clause without whitelisting or parameterization, and OrderCriteria objects like this are typically populated from user-supplied sort/filter parameters in admin list endpoints, allowing HQL injection via the order-by field name.

criteria.getCriteriaOrderByField() is concatenated directly into the HQL string for the ORDER BY clause with no whitelist validation or escaping, and unlike the code/name parameters it is never bound via setParameter — if this field originates from user-controllable request input (typical for merchant search/sort criteria), it allows HQL/order-by injection.

The promo code is inserted as a plain fact field into a Drools KieSession that runs a fixed, classpath-bundled rule file (PromoCoupon.drl); it is not compiled or evaluated as code itself. Without evidence that the DRL rules dynamically eval the promoCode string, this is fact-based rule matching, not code injection.

The discount value is already null-checked at line 88 before use, and this line merely reads a Double field into a local variable with no untrusted input parsing or unsanitized data flowing to a dangerous sink. Nothing in the shown code demonstrates missing validation of externally-controlled input at this specific line.

This code builds an HTTP request body (form-encoded parameters) for a payment gateway API call, not a SQL query — there is no database interaction anywhere in this snippet, so CWE-89 (SQL Injection) is a misclassification of the sink type.

The flagged line is merely the method signature; the body just tokenizes a payload into URL-decoded key/value pairs and stores them in a map with no unsafe sink (no SQL, command exec, file I/O, or reflection) shown in this snippet. Without evidence of the values being used unsafely downstream, this looks like a generic CWE-20 flag on ordinary parsing logic rather than a concrete input-validation vulnerability.

A plain `new Digester()` is used to parse the USPS API response body without disabling DTD processing or external entity resolution (no setFeature calls disabling external-general/parameter entities), and Apache Commons Digester's underlying SAX parser is XXE-vulnerable by default. Since the XML being parsed comes from an external HTTP response, this is a genuine unhardened XML parser processing externally-sourced data.

The code uses the deprecated single-argument URLEncoder.encode(String), which silently relies on the JVM's platform-default charset instead of an explicit encoding like UTF-8, matching the CWE-176 improper-encoding-handling pattern; this can produce inconsistent or corrupted encoding of the XML request across platforms/locales, though practical security impact here is low since the encoded data is an internally-built XML request rather than directly attacker-controlled input.

digester.parse() parses XML data from an external UPS API response without any visible disabling of DOCTYPE/external-entity processing, and Apache Commons Digester (backed by SAX) is XXE-vulnerable by default unless explicitly hardened. No secure-parsing configuration (e.g., disallow-doctype-decl, external-general-entities=false) is shown in this code or visible context.

The IV for AES/CBC encryption is a hardcoded, static string constant, making ciphertext deterministic and vulnerable to pattern-analysis/known-plaintext style attacks; this is a classic hardcoded/predictable IV weakness (better classified as CWE-329) rather than the stated CWE-328, but it is a genuine cryptographic defect in production code.

Line 19 is merely a field declaration (`private String secretKey;`) with no assigned value shown; the actual hardcoded value in this file is IV_P (a static IV, not a credential), and there's no evidence secretKey is hardcoded rather than injected via constructor/setter.

The temp file prefix is built from a product ID (numeric) and the suffix 'extension' is used later as an ImageIO format name (ImageIO.write), implying it's a validated/whitelisted image format string rather than raw attacker-controlled path input; File.createTempFile also always places the file under the system temp directory using JVM-generated random names, so there's no evident attacker-controlled path segment enabling traversal from the code shown.

The flagged line only extracts a substring from a content-type string derived via URLConnection's built-in FileNameMap (a fixed extension→MIME mapping), with no file write, upload storage, or execution occurring at this line; there's no evidence of unrestricted dangerous-type file upload in the shown code.

The `tmpl` parameter is concatenated into a template path passed to FreeMarker's getTemplate, but nothing in the shown code indicates it originates from untrusted/user-controlled HTTP input rather than an internal, developer-chosen template name (e.g., order confirmation, password reset templates) typical of this email-sending module; without visibility into the caller chain this looks like a mis-classified sink on an internal constant rather than a reachable path traversal.

The flagged line is a normal DataSource.getInputStream() implementation returning a ByteArrayInputStream; the adjacent commented-out lines are inert dead code, not active debug functionality, and CWE-489 does not apply here.

The code concatenates email.getTemplateName() unsanitized into a template path passed to Freemarker's getTemplate(), a classic path-traversal-prone pattern if the name is attacker-influenced, but the shown snippet doesn't reveal where getTemplateName() originates (often a hardcoded internal identifier rather than raw user input) so reachability from untrusted input can't be confirmed.

The flagged line is only the method signature with null-checks; the actual file path construction (likely in addFile() or the underlying repository, using contentFile.getFileName()) is not shown, so it cannot be determined whether the filename is sanitized before being used in a file path.

The lookup is already scoped by store.getId() alongside the cart id, meaning cross-tenant access is constrained by the repository query itself rather than left unchecked. No unscoped or user-controllable access path is visible in this snippet to support a genuine access-control defect.

This is a simple repository lookup scoped by store ID and cart code inside a service method; no authorization logic is expected or missing at this layer, and access control (if any) would be enforced at the controller/session layer, which is not shown here. Nothing in the shown code indicates a broken or missing access-control check.

The X-Forwarded-Host request header is fully attacker-controlled and is assigned directly to the returned URI with no validation, encoding, or CRLF stripping; given the method's purpose (building a forwarded store URI, likely used in a redirect/Location or canonical header downstream), this is a classic unsanitized-header-to-header-sink pattern for CWE-113/response splitting.

The method takes a raw HTTP request parameter value and binds it directly into an arbitrary object field via Spring's forDirectFieldAccess PropertyAccessor, bypassing any setter validation/sanitization — the textbook pattern for CWE-915 mass assignment. Even though the property name (setterName) is a caller-supplied argument rather than shown as directly request-derived, the value is unvalidated attacker-controlled input written straight into internal object state via reflection.

The loop only iterates over keys of mappingFields, which is a caller-supplied whitelist of allowed field mappings rather than raw attacker-controlled parameter names, so this dispatch line itself doesn't directly expose mass-assignment; the actual risk (if any) would be inside the unseen setValue reflection logic, not this line.

This encrypts data using a statically-defined Cipher string with no IV/nonce parameter passed to init(), and in the known Shopizer codebase this CIPHER constant is "AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding" — ECB mode is a broken/risky cryptographic algorithm choice, consistent with CWE-327.

The key is generated at runtime via KeyGenerator.generateKey(), not a hardcoded/embedded constant, so CWE-321 (use of hard-coded cryptographic key) is a mis-classification; the actual weakness here is use of the broken DES algorithm (CWE-327/326), not a hardcoded key.

imageName and extension are concatenated directly into a filename passed to contentService.getContentFile with no visible sanitization or path traversal checks (e.g. rejecting '../'), and these values are typical Spring @PathVariable/@RequestParam inputs from the request URL, making traversal plausible if the service resolves them to a filesystem path.

The filter reflects the untrusted Origin request header directly into Access-Control-Allow-Origin without any allow-list validation, meaning any origin is trusted; combined with the broad allowed headers/methods this is a classic overly permissive CORS misconfiguration (CWE-942), risky especially if credentials are also allowed elsewhere.

The method unconditionally returns true, bypassing both the password-reset-timing check and token-expiration check that are computed but discarded (with the real logic commented out below); this means any token, even one issued before a password reset or expired, will be considered refreshable, defeating authentication/session invalidation guarantees.

The filter reflects the untrusted Origin request header verbatim into Access-Control-Allow-Origin while also setting Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, allowing any origin to make authenticated cross-site requests and read responses — a textbook permissive CORS misconfiguration.

The redirect target is `request.getContextPath()` (fixed by the servlet container, not user input) concatenated with `accessDeniedUrl`, a server-side configured field set via setter (Spring bean property), not derived from any request parameter or user-controlled input. No untrusted data flows into sendRedirect here.

The code merely populates and persists a ProductReview entity via a populator/service call with no permission or privilege-handling logic visible; CWE-280 (Improper Handling of Insufficient Permissions or Privileges) does not apply to this line, indicating a mis-classification rather than a real vulnerability.

The flagged line is simply a for-loop iterating over availabilities to update price fields; there is no access-control logic (or absence thereof) visible in this snippet that would constitute CWE-284, suggesting a mis-classification by the tool rather than an authorization defect in this code.

OrderStatus.valueOf(status) can throw IllegalArgumentException on an invalid string, but this endpoint is gated by authorizeUser requiring SUPERADMIN/ADMIN/ADMIN_ORDER/ADMIN_RETAIL roles, so it's not reachable by untrusted input; worst case is an uncaught exception from an already-privileged admin, not a genuine exploitable defect.

The flagged line only instantiates an OrderCriteria object; no SQL query construction or execution occurs here or in the visible surrounding code. Parameters are simply set on a criteria bean, with no evidence of string-concatenated SQL at this location.

The endpoint is under /private/user/profile, implying it's gated by Spring Security authentication, so request.getUserPrincipal() should not return null when this handler executes; there's no untrusted input here, at most a latent NPE risk if security config is misconfigured, not an input-validation vulnerability.

merchantStore is already dereferenced via merchantStore.getCode() at line 189 before reaching line 194, so it cannot be null here; furthermore string concatenation with a null object in Java safely converts to "null" rather than throwing an NPE.

The shown code only passes the validated ContactForm and request context to emailTemplatesUtils.sendContactEmail(); whether that sink safely escapes/encodes the user-supplied fields (e.g., in an email template or headers) is not visible here, so injection cannot be confirmed or ruled out from this snippet alone.

The catch-all handler returns e.getMessage() directly in the HTTP response body, exposing internal exception details (potentially including implementation specifics or stack info) to the client, which is a textbook CWE-209 information exposure through error messages.

This code is exception handling for authentication failure, returning a 401 with a generic message — it has nothing to do with permission assignment on a resource (CWE-732), indicating a rule misclassification rather than a real defect.
